title: Chapter 12: The Exchange
content: Nether-Grid Exchange. A city in a pocket dimension. Built on contracts and debt.
The Exchange wasn't what Roxanne expected.

She'd imagined a dark, demonic marketplace — fire and brimstone, screaming souls, the whole mythology package. Instead, the Exchange looked like the world's most aggressive financial district. Skyscrapers made of black glass. Holographic tickers streaming price data in languages that predated humanity. Streets filled with beings in business attire, moving with the purpose of commuters on deadline.
It was Wall Street. But every transaction was a soul.

"Welcome to the Exchange," the auditor said. It had been re-assigned as Roxanne's guide. "Please don't touch anything. Don't make eye contact with anyone for more than three seconds. Don't accept any drinks. Don't sign anything without reading it three times."
"That's a lot of rules."
"The Exchange runs on consent. But consent can be... engineered. A handshake is a contract here. A nod is an agreement. A smile can be a binding offer."
Roxanne tucked her hands into her pockets. "Got it. Don't smile. Don't nod. Don't shake hands."
"And don't buy anything without consulting me first."
"That one I'm definitely ignoring."
The auditor said nothing.

Silas walked beside her, his red eyes scanning the crowd. He'd drawn his shadow-coat tighter around himself, hiding his features. "There are watchers," he murmured. "Faces I recognize. Old contractors. They're noting your arrival."
"Let them note."
They approached the Central Spire — a building that pierced the violet sky, its surface covered in scrolling text. Contracts. Millions of them, rendered in light, updating in real-time.
And waiting at the entrance was a woman.

She was beautiful in the way a blade is beautiful. Sharp cheekbones. Hair the color of silver smoke. Eyes that held the cold calculation of someone who had been playing this game for a very long time.
"Roxanne Vance," the woman said. "I'm Director Vasquez. Nether-Grid Compliance, Central Division. I'm the one who sent the audit request."
"You're the supervisor."

"I'm the supervisor's supervisor. The auditor's report was... interesting. A newly awakened Primordial card. The release of a Bound God. The acquisition of an entire university campus. And then the purchase of three Compliance Division employees."
She smiled. It didn't reach her eyes.
"You've been busy."
"I'm an overachiever."
"So I see." Vasquez gestured toward the spire. "Come inside. We need to discuss your account status."
"My account status?"

"The Primordial card you're holding isn't registered. It has no account number. It has no transaction history — or rather, its transaction history goes back twelve thousand years and then stops. According to our records, the last registered transaction was a withdrawal in 9,847 BCE. After that, the account went dormant."
"Until three days ago."
"Until three days ago. A dormant Primordial account waking up after twelve millennia, with an infinite balance, is... irregular. The system needs to categorize it."
"Categorize it how?"
Vasquez's smile widened.
"That's what we're going to discuss."
She followed Vasquez into the Central Spire.

The interior was a single vast atrium, stretching upward so far that the ceiling was lost in haze. Floating platforms moved between levels, carrying beings in various states of corporeality. The walls were covered in contract screens — endless streams of text in a hundred languages.
Vasquez led them to a conference room on what she called the "Medium-Value Floor." The room had a glass table, floating chairs, and a view of the Exchange's skyline.
"Sit."
Roxanne sat. Silas remained standing behind her.
Vasquez sat across from them. She placed a tablet on the table — the screen glowed with a familiar symbol: the Primordial seal from Roxanne's card.

"Your card is a Class-1 Primordial artifact. That means it predates the current Nether-Grid regulatory framework by approximately six billion years. It's not illegal to hold it. But it is... unclassified."
"Unclassified?"

"The Nether-Grid has transaction categories. Personal. Commercial. Sovereign. Divine. Your card doesn't fit any of them. It's too powerful for personal use, too ancient for commercial registration, too independent for sovereign oversight, and too human-held for divine classification."
"So what do you want to do with it?"
Vasquez leaned forward.

"I want to give it a category before someone else decides to confiscate it. There are factions in the Upper Nether-Grid who would very much like to own a Primordial artifact. If your card remains unclassified, they have legal grounds to challenge your ownership."
"I bought it. It's mine."
"You inherited it. There's a difference. Inheritance can be contested. Especially when the bloodline was believed extinct."
She slid the tablet across the table.

"Sign this. It classifies your card as a Sovereign Account, which grants you the same legal standing as a minor god. Protection from confiscation. Access to the Exchange's high-value market. And recognition as a legitimate power in the Nether-Grid."
Roxanne read the contract. It was dense. But she understood the fundamental structure: sign this, get protection. Don't sign this, stay vulnerable.
"What's the catch?"
"The catch is that Sovereign Accounts are taxable. You'll owe 2% of your monthly transaction volume in Nether-Grid tax. Standard rate."
"Two percent of infinity is still infinity."
"Technically correct. But it establishes a paper trail. And a paper trail means legitimacy."
Roxanne picked up the tablet. Read it once more.
Then she signed.
The tablet chimed. Green light.

"Congratulations," Vasquez said. "You are now a recognized Sovereign Account holder in the Nether-Grid Exchange. Your territory is protected under Article 7 of the Exchange Charter. Your card cannot be seized without a formal adjudication hearing."
"That's it?"
"That's it for now. But I should warn you: the Upper Nether-Grid knows about you now. The registration of a Sovereign Account is public knowledge. There will be... interest."
"What kind of interest?"
"The kind that comes with offers. Demands. Threats. You have something they want. They will try to get it."
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"Let them try."
*End of Chapter 12*
They left the Spire with a newly registered account, a tax rate, and a target on their backs.
"That was too easy," Roxanne said as they walked through the Exchange plaza.
"It was," Silas agreed. "Vasquez was genuine, but she's not the only player. The registration went through too cleanly. Someone upstairs smoothed the path."
"Someone who wants something."
"Someone who wants you to owe them a favor."
Roxanne stopped. Looked up at the towering spires of the Exchange. Somewhere up there, someone had just done her a favor she hadn't asked for.
She hated owing people.
"We need to find out who," she said. "Before they come to collect."
"Agreed."
A figure stepped out of the crowd. Human-shaped. Middle-aged. Wearing a suit that looked like it cost more than most people's souls.
"Ms. Vance." He held out a card. Not a weapon — a business card. "My employer would like to meet you. At your convenience."
The card read:
